content:
12 min read
Just now

--

The model was never the bottleneck. Your prompt was.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Most developers use AI the exact same way, no matter which model is sitting behind the chat window. They open a ticket, paste in the requirements, and type some version of: write this endpoint, write this service, write this migration.
Something comes back, it compiles, someone approves the PR, and everyone moves on with their day.
On the surface, that looks like progress. Faster typing, fewer syntax errors, less boilerplate to write by hand. But if you’ve spent real time using these models on production systems (backend services, distributed jobs, integrations with third-party vendors) at some point you notice something uncomfortable: the code was never the hard part. Not before AI, and not after. The hard part was always the fifty small decisions that happen before a single line gets written, and that’s exactly the part most people skip when they hand a ticket straight to a model.
Ask a senior engineer what actually eats up their week, and it’s almost never typing. It’s deciding whether a service should own its own data or read from another team’s store. It’s about figuring out what happens when a downstream call times out halfway through a batch. It’s finding the one business rule buried in a Confluence page from two years ago that nobody remembers writing. Generating code doesn’t touch any of that. It solves the part that, out of the whole lifecycle, was already the cheapest.
The habit everyone picked up without noticing
I don’t think this is a discipline problem. I think it’s a product design problem. Every mainstream AI coding tool is built around the same interaction: you describe an outcome, and it produces code. That’s the shape of the product, so that’s the shape of the habit that formed around it.
The result is that most of us (myself included, for a good while) ended up using a frontier reasoning model as a very expensive autocomplete. You’re handing a system capable of holding an architecture discussion the job of “type faster,” and then wondering why the code it hands back needs three rounds of review before anyone’s comfortable shipping it.
At this point, I strongly believe that it’s something the entire community had to go through, for better or worse. It’s like going back to being children, when we have to understand how things work and find the right path, the most efficient way to achieve something.
Don’t ask a baby to run, they won’t. They’ll find other ways (crawling, creeping, or even crying). Suddenly, they’ll realize that walking uses less energy and allows you to reach things that are higher.
Well, we are the babies now, and we are growing up.
I made a version of this point when I wrote about technical debt in agile teams: most debt doesn’t come from bad code. It comes from decisions made too fast, without enough context, that nobody revisits until they break something. AI doesn’t fix that pattern by writing the code faster. It speeds it up. A decision made in five seconds is still a decision made in five seconds, whether a person typed it or a model did.
Where the leverage actually is
Here’s the part that matters, and it has nothing to do with which model you’re using.
Software has a lifecycle: requirements, design, tradeoff analysis, implementation, testing, review, production, incident, postmortem. Generating code lives almost entirely in one stage… implementation. It’s the stage with the least ambiguity left, because by the time you get there, someone has already made every architectural call that matters.
The leverage sits earlier. Design is where ambiguity is highest, where a wrong call is cheapest to walk back, and where a second opinion (even a probabilistic one) is worth the most. Once the code exists, you’re debugging a decision. Before it exists, you can still change that decision for free.
So instead of: “Write this API.”
I’ve started asking things like:

• How should this actually be designed, given what we know now and what’s likely to change in six months?

• What are the tradeoffs between the two or three approaches that could work here?

• What security problems does this design open up that I’m not seeing?

• What business rules am I probably forgetting, based on similar systems?

• What architecture actually fits this specific load pattern, and not the generic textbook answer?

• What can fail in production once this is running at real scale?

• What should I be testing that isn’t obvious from the happy path?

• How would a staff engineer review this before signing off?

• How would you try to break this if you were on the other side of the review?

• Is this overengineered for what we actually need right now?

• What am I assuming here that I haven’t said out loud?
None of these produces a single line of code. All of them change what code eventually gets written, and they change it before it costs anything to change.
A concrete example, because abstractions don’t convince anyone
Picture a pricing service that has to keep tens of thousands of SKUs in sync across hundreds of physical locations, fed by an external vendor system you have no control over. This is exactly the kind of system where “write me the ingestion endpoint” is a terrible first prompt, because the endpoint is the least interesting part of the problem.
The interesting part shows up once you ask the design questions instead. What happens when the vendor sends a partial batch and the connection drops halfway through? What happens when the same record shows up twice, three minutes apart, because they retried something on their end? What happens when a downstream system sends back something that looks like an error but is actually a normal outcome you need to treat as a success?
That last one isn’t hypothetical. I hit exactly that in production once: an integration where “this record already exists” was, by design, a success signal, not a failure. Skip the design conversation and jump straight to “write the error handling for this endpoint,” and the model does the obvious, reasonable, wrong thing. It treats the duplicate as an error because that’s usually what “error handling” means. Nobody told it otherwise, because nobody had that conversation before the code existed.
That’s not a model limitation. That’s a design gap implementation can’t patch, because the gap was never there to begin with.
Using the model as a reviewer, not just a producer
The most useful habit I’ve built isn’t a better prompt for writing code. It’s treating the model as an adversarial reviewer before the design is even settled.
When I broke down the SDD harness I run day to day, the propose and spec phases exist for exactly this reason. They’re not there to produce code faster. They’re there because that’s where the real risk in a feature actually lives, and once a spec is wrong, every phase after it inherits the mistake. The phase that actually writes the code is almost boring by comparison — most of the expensive thinking already happened.
You can get a version of this without any harness at all. Take a design you’re fairly confident in and ask it directly: how would you break this? Not “review this,” which just invites a polite, surface-level pass. Ask it to attack the design the way an actual incident would. What happens under partial failure? Under concurrent writes? Under a dependency that’s slow instead of fully down? You’ll get answers ranging from irrelevant to genuinely uncomfortable, and the uncomfortable ones are the ones worth sitting with.
I wrote something similar about designing MCP servers as interfaces for agents rather than APIs for humans: the quality of an interface isn’t measured by how flexible it is, it’s measured by how hard it is to misuse. The same logic applies to how you use the model itself. A loose prompt that just says “write the code” hands the model maximum freedom to guess at your intent. A structured design conversation pushes it toward the one thing you actually need: a decision you can defend later, in a postmortem, to someone who wasn’t in the room.
The overengineering question nobody asks the model
There’s one specific failure worth calling out on its own: asking a model to design something and getting back way more architecture than the problem actually deserves.
Models are trained on a lot of textbook system design, and textbook system design tends to assume a scale and set of failure modes you might not have yet. Ask for “a resilient, event-driven architecture for user signups” and you might get something built to handle ten thousand requests a second when you’re doing forty a minute.
That’s exactly why “Is this overengineered for what we need right now?” belongs in the loop as its own explicit question, not something you just assume gets checked. A senior engineer catches this almost by instinct because they’ve felt the cost of an unnecessary queue, an unnecessary cache layer, an abstraction that exists purely to handle a scenario that will never come close to happening at your actual scale. A model doesn’t feel that cost. Someone has to ask.
Security and testing deserve their own design pass
Two of the questions on that list rarely get asked early enough, even by engineers who are otherwise disciplined about design: what security problems does this open up, and what should I actually be testing?
Security tends to get treated like a checklist you run after the design is already locked in. Someone builds the service, then someone else (maybe a security review, maybe a linter) checks it against the usual suspects. But most of the security problems that actually matter aren’t injection strings or a missing header. They’re boundary problems, baked straight into the design. Who’s allowed to call this endpoint and under what identity? What happens if a request gets replayed? Whether a batch job processing records on behalf of one location accidentally has enough privilege to touch every other location’s data too, because nobody scoped the permission tightly enough when the design was drawn up. You don’t catch that in code review. You catch it, if you’re lucky, in a pentest. Or you catch it in an incident.
Asking a model what security issues exist in a design before you’ve built anything gets you a genuinely useful first pass: identity boundaries, replay risk, what happens if this component gets compromised, what the blast radius looks like if a credential leaks. It won’t be exhaustive. It doesn’t need to be. It just needs to surface the two or three things you hadn’t thought of yet, and it’s remarkably good at that when you feed it the actual design instead of a vague description of the feature.
Testing has the same problem, just flipped. Ask for tests after the code already exists, and you get tests that mirror the code, which mostly just confirm the code does what the code does. Ask for a testing strategy during design, and you get something more useful: what needs a unit test, what needs a test that simulates a slow dependency rather than a missing one, what needs a concurrency test because two instances of this job might run at the same time under real deployment conditions, what needs an idempotency test because retries are going to happen eventually whether you plan for them or not.
For the pricing-sync example, the useful test isn’t “Does the endpoint return 200 for a valid payload?” It’s “what happens if the exact same batch shows up twice, five seconds apart, because a load balancer retried due to a timeout.” That test only gets written if someone asked the question before implementation, not after.
What changes when you move the model earlier
Something pretty practical happens once you start front-loading the design conversation instead of jumping straight to implementation: the code that eventually gets written needs a lot fewer rounds of review.
Not because the model writes cleaner syntax. Because the ambiguity that us
